title: GCD using recursion
description: Find greatest common divisor using Euclidean algorithm recursively

examples.explanation: Step 1: min(12,18) = 12. Initialize result = 1. Step 2: i=1: 12%1=0, 18%1=0 → result=1. Step 3: i=2: 12%2=0, 18%2=0 → result=2. Step 4: i=3: 12%3=0, 18%3=0 → result=3. Step 5: i=4: 12%4=0, 18%4=2 (not 0) → no update. Step 6: i=5: 12%5=2, 18%5=3 → no update. Step 7: i=6: 12%6=0, 18%6=0 → result=6. Step 8: i=7 to 12: no updates. Final result = 6.

Step 1: min(17,13) = 13. Initialize result = 1. Step 2: i=1: 17%1=0, 13%1=0 → result=1. Step 3: i=2: 17%2=1 (not 0) → no update. Step 4: i=3: 17%3=2 → no update. Step 5: i=4: 17%4=1 → no update. Step 6: i=5: 17%5=2 → no update. Step 7: i=6: 17%6=5 → no update. Step 8: i=7: 17%7=3 → no update. Step 9: i=8: 17%8=1 → no update. Step 10: i=9: 17%9=8 → no update. Step 11: i=10: 17%10=7 → no update. Step 12: i=11: 17%11=6 → no update. Step 13: i=12: 17%12=5 → no update. Step 14: i=13: 17%13=4 → no update. Final result = 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(min(a,b))
approaches.summary: Iterate from 1 to the smaller number, checking divisibility of both numbers to find the largest common divisor. Use a loop to test all possible divisors.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force GCD Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of GCD using brute force iteration from 1 to min(a,b)
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two numbers a and b works by systematically testing all possible divisors from 1 up to the smaller of the two numbers. The intuition is simple: any common divisor must divide both numbers, and since we're looking for the greatest one, we can start from the smaller number and work downward, or start from 1 and work upward while keeping track of the maximum found. The algorithm iterates through all integers i from 1 to min(a,b), checking if both a and b are divisible by i (i.e., a % i == 0 and b % i == 0). The largest i satisfying this condition is the GCD. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large numbers because it examines every possible divisor. Time complexity is O(min(a,b)) since we perform min(a,b) iterations, each with constant-time operations. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) by implementing the complete loop structure. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting the loop from 0 (causing division by zero), 2) Using <= instead of < in loop conditions, 3) Forgetting to handle negative numbers (GCD is defined for positive integers, so take absolute values), 4) Not initializing the result variable properly. The real code pattern involves a for loop with modulus checks and a variable to track the maximum divisor.

examples.trace: Step 1: min(12,18) = 12. Initialize result = 1. Step 2: i=1: 12%1=0, 18%1=0 → result=1. Step 3: i=2: 12%2=0, 18%2=0 → result=2. Step 4: i=3: 12%3=0, 18%3=0 → result=3. Step 5: i=4: 12%4=0, 18%4=2 (not 0) → no update. Step 6: i=5: 12%5=2, 18%5=3 → no update. Step 7: i=6: 12%6=0, 18%6=0 → result=6. Step 8: i=7 to 12: no updates. Final result = 6.

Step 1: min(17,13) = 13. Initialize result = 1. Step 2: i=1: 17%1=0, 13%1=0 → result=1. Step 3: i=2: 17%2=1 (not 0) → no update. Step 4: i=3: 17%3=2 → no update. Step 5: i=4: 17%4=1 → no update. Step 6: i=5: 17%5=2 → no update. Step 7: i=6: 17%6=5 → no update. Step 8: i=7: 17%7=3 → no update. Step 9: i=8: 17%8=1 → no update. Step 10: i=9: 17%9=8 → no update. Step 11: i=10: 17%10=7 → no update. Step 12: i=11: 17%11=6 → no update. Step 13: i=12: 17%12=5 → no update. Step 14: i=13: 17%13=4 → no update. Final result = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Divisor Checking Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the loop that checks divisibility for all numbers from 1 to n
ladders.explanation: The key subroutine in the brute force GCD approach is the loop that iterates through all potential divisors and checks divisibility. The intuition is that to find common divisors, we need to test each candidate number i to see if it divides both input numbers without remainder. This is done using the modulus operator (%), which returns the remainder of division. The algorithm takes two numbers a and b and an upper limit n (which is min(a,b)), then loops i from 1 to n. For each i, it checks if a % i == 0 and b % i == 0. If both conditions are true, i is a common divisor. We typically track the maximum such i found. Time complexity is O(n) where n = min(a,b), as we perform n iterations with constant work each. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem (L0) by providing the core iteration mechanism. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors in loop range (use i <= n or i < n+1), 2) Not handling the case when n=0 (if either input is 0, GCD is the other number), 3) Using integer division instead of modulus. The real code pattern is a simple for loop with if statements checking modulus results.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=1: 8%1=0, 12%1=0 → common divisor. Step 2: i=2: 8%2=0, 12%2=0 → common divisor. Step 3: i=3: 8%3=2, 12%3=0 → not common. Step 4: i=4: 8%4=0, 12%4=0 → common divisor. Step 5: i=5: 8%5=3, 12%5=2 → not common. Step 6: i=6: 8%6=2, 12%6=0 → not common. Step 7: i=7: 8%7=1, 12%7=5 → not common. Step 8: i=8: 8%8=0, 12%8=4 → not common. Common divisors found: 1,2,4.

Step 1: i=1: 5%1=0, 7%1=0 → common divisor. Step 2: i=2: 5%2=1, 7%2=1 → not common. Step 3: i=3: 5%3=2, 7%3=1 → not common. Step 4: i=4: 5%4=1, 7%4=3 → not common. Step 5: i=5: 5%5=0, 7%5=2 → not common. Only common divisor: 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Maximum Common Divisor
ladders.desc: Algorithm to track and return the largest number that divides both inputs
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force GCD approach is the process of identifying and tracking the maximum common divisor while iterating through potential divisors. The intuition is that since we're checking divisors in increasing order (from 1 upward), whenever we find a new common divisor, it might be larger than previously found ones, so we need to update our maximum. Alternatively, we could iterate downward from min(a,b) to 1 and return the first common divisor found, which would be more efficient but still O(min(a,b)). The algorithm maintains a variable 'result' initialized to 1 (since 1 always divides any integer). For each i from 1 to min(a,b), if i divides both a and b, we set result = i. After the loop, result contains the GCD. Time complexity is O(min(a,b)) for the iteration. Space is O(1). This connects to the key subroutine (L1) by adding the maximum tracking mechanism. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to initialize result (should be 1, not 0), 2) Updating result even when i doesn't divide both numbers, 3) Not considering that iterating upward requires checking all numbers (whereas downward can stop early). The real code pattern involves initializing max_divisor = 1, then in the loop: if a%i==0 and b%i==0: max_divisor = i.

examples.trace: Step 1: min=12, result=1. Step 2: i=1: divides both → result=1. Step 3: i=2: divides both → result=2. Step 4: i=3: 12%3=0, 16%3=1 → no update. Step 5: i=4: divides both → result=4. Step 6: i=5: 12%5=2 → no update. Step 7: i=6: 12%6=0, 16%6=4 → no update. Step 8: i=7 to 12: no updates. Final result=4.

Step 1: min=9, result=1. Step 2: i=1: divides both → result=1. Step 3: i=2: 9%2=1 → no update. Step 4: i=3: divides both → result=3. Step 5: i=4: 9%4=1 → no update. Step 6: i=5: 9%5=4 → no update. Step 7: i=6: 9%6=3 → no update. Step 8: i=7: 9%7=2 → no update. Step 9: i=8: 9%8=1 → no update. Step 10: i=9: divides both → result=9. Final result=9.

ladders.title: Building Block — Divisibility Check for Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Check if a given number divides two other numbers without remainder
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on checking whether a candidate divisor 'd' divides both numbers 'a' and 'b' without leaving a remainder. The intuition is based on the definition of divisibility: a number d divides another number n if n % d == 0, meaning the remainder is zero when n is divided by d. For d to be a common divisor of a and b, it must satisfy both a % d == 0 and b % d == 0. The algorithm takes three inputs: a, b, and d. It computes the remainders r1 = a % d and r2 = b % d, then returns true if both r1 and r2 are zero, false otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant-time modulus operations. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the core logic (L2) by providing the fundamental check used in each iteration of the loop. Common mistakes: 1) Using division (/) instead of modulus (%), 2) Not handling d=0 (division by zero error), 3) Checking a/d and b/d are integers instead of using modulus. The real code pattern is a simple boolean expression: return (a % d == 0) and (b % d == 0).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 12 % 6 = 0. Step 2: Compute 18 % 6 = 0. Step 3: Both remainders are 0, so d=6 divides both numbers. Return true.
Step 1: Compute 12 % 5 = 2. Step 2: Compute 18 % 5 = 3. Step 3: Remainders are not 0 (2 and 3), so d=5 does not divide both. Return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulus (Remainder) Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another
ladders.explanation: The modulus operation, denoted by % in many programming languages, computes the remainder when one integer (dividend) is divided by another (divisor). The intuition is that division of integers often doesn't result in a whole number; the remainder is what's left over. Formally, for integers a and d (d ≠ 0), a % d = r where 0 ≤ r < |d|, and there exists an integer q such that a = d*q + r. This operation is fundamental for checking divisibility: if a % d == 0, then d divides a evenly. The algorithm takes two numbers a and d, and returns the remainder. In implementation, most languages provide % as a built-in operator. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to the building block (L3) as it's used to check remainders for divisibility. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing modulus with integer division (which gives quotient), 2) Not handling negative numbers correctly (different languages handle negatives differently; for GCD, we typically use absolute values), 3) Using d=0 causing division by zero. The real code pattern is simply using the % operator: remainder = a % d.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide 17 by 5. Step 2: 5*3 = 15, remainder = 17 - 15 = 2. So 17 % 5 = 2.
Step 1: Divide 20 by 4. Step 2: 4*5 = 20, remainder = 20 - 20 = 0. So 20 % 4 = 0, meaning 4 divides 20 evenly.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Division and Factors
ladders.desc: Understand how integers divide each other and what factors/divisors are
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the basic idea of integer division and factors. The intuition is that when we divide one integer by another, we get a quotient and possibly a remainder. If the remainder is zero, we say the divisor is a factor (or divisor) of the dividend. For example, 4 is a factor of 12 because 12 ÷ 4 = 3 with remainder 0. The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two numbers is the largest integer that is a factor of both. To build towards the brute force GCD approach, we need to understand that to find common factors, we can test all possible divisors. The algorithm concept: given two numbers, list all factors of each and find the largest common one. Time complexity depends on how we list factors; brute force checks all numbers up to the smaller number. Space is O(1). This connects to the basic operation (L4) as modulus helps check for zero remainder. Common mistakes: 1) Thinking factors can be fractions (they must be integers), 2) Confusing factors with multiples, 3) Forgetting that 1 and the number itself are always factors. The real code pattern involves understanding that a % d == 0 means d is a factor of a.

examples.trace: Step 1: Test 1: 12%1=0 → factor. Step 2: Test 2: 12%2=0 → factor. Step 3: Test 3: 12%3=0 → factor. Step 4: Test 4: 12%4=0 → factor. Step 5: Test 5: 12%5=2 → not factor. Step 6: Test 6: 12%6=0 → factor. Step 7: Test 7 to 11: not factors. Step 8: Test 12: 12%12=0 → factor. Factors are 1,2,3,4,6,12.

Step 1: Test 1: 7%1=0 → factor. Step 2: Test 2: 7%2=1 → not factor. Step 3: Test 3: 7%3=1 → not factor. Step 4: Test 4: 7%4=3 → not factor. Step 5: Test 5: 7%5=2 → not factor. Step 6: Test 6: 7%6=1 → not factor. Step 7: Test 7: 7%7=0 → factor. Factors are 1 and 7 (prime number).

testCases.expected: Special: all numbers are factors except 0 itself

approaches.name: Euclidean Algorithm Recursive O(log(min(a,b)))
approaches.summary: Apply the Euclidean algorithm recursively: if b is 0, return a; otherwise, recursively compute gcd(b, a % b). This reduces the problem size by using the modulo operation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive GCD Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Euclidean algorithm using recursion to find greatest common divisor
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires implementing the Euclidean algorithm recursively to find the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers. The intuition behind this approach is based on the mathematical property that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This works because any divisor of both a and b must also divide their difference, and by extension, the remainder when a is divided by b. The algorithm reduces the problem size at each recursive call until the base case is reached. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Define function gcd(a, b). 2. If b == 0, return a (base case). 3. Otherwise, return gcd(b, a % b). Time complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) because each recursive call reduces the larger number significantly (at least by half in worst case). Space complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) due to recursion stack depth. This connects directly to the key subroutine by implementing the complete recursive logic. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (should take absolute values), not ensuring a >= b (algorithm works regardless), and infinite recursion with non-integer inputs. The real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): if b == 0: return abs(a) return gcd(b, a % b).

examples.trace:
Step 1: gcd(48, 18) → b=18 ≠ 0, so call gcd(18, 48%18=12)
Step 2: gcd(18, 12) → b=12 ≠ 0, so call gcd(12, 18%12=6)
Step 3: gcd(12, 6) → b=6 ≠ 0, so call gcd(6, 12%6=0)
Step 4: gcd(6, 0) → b=0, return 6
Final: 6

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Modulo Reduction
ladders.desc: The recursive function that applies gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b) repeatedly
ladders.explanation: This key subroutine implements the core recursive logic of the Euclidean algorithm. The intuition is that instead of computing GCD directly, we transform the problem into a smaller equivalent problem using the modulo operation. Mathematically, gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b) because any common divisor of a and b must divide a - q*b (where q = floor(a/b)), which is exactly a mod b. The algorithm: 1. Receive parameters a and b. 2. If b == 0, return a. 3. Otherwise, compute a % b and recursively call with (b, a % b). Time complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) as each call reduces the numbers. Space complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) for recursion stack. This feeds into the full problem by being the complete recursive implementation—the full problem just calls this function. Common mistakes include not handling the base case correctly, not using absolute values for negative numbers, and not understanding that the algorithm works regardless of whether a >= b. Real code pattern: def gcd_recursive(a, b): if b == 0: return a return gcd_recursive(b, a % b).

examples.trace:
Step 1: gcd_recursive(30, 12) → b=12 ≠ 0, call gcd_recursive(12, 30%12=6)
Step 2: gcd_recursive(12, 6) → b=6 ≠ 0, call gcd_recursive(6, 12%6=0)
Step 3: gcd_recursive(6, 0) → b=0, return 6
Final: 6

Step 1: gcd_recursive(56, 98) → b=98 ≠ 0, call gcd_recursive(98, 56%98=56)
Step 2: gcd_recursive(98, 56) → b=56 ≠ 0, call gcd_recursive(56, 98%56=42)
Step 3: gcd_recursive(56, 42) → b=42 ≠ 0, call gcd_recursive(42, 56%42=14)
Step 4: gcd_recursive(42, 14) → b=14 ≠ 0, call gcd_recursive(14, 42%14=0)
Step 5: gcd_recursive(14, 0) → return 14
Final: 14

ladders.title: Core Logic — Euclidean Algorithm Property
ladders.desc: Understanding that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b)
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the mathematical insight that makes the recursive approach work. The intuition comes from the divisibility property: if d divides both a and b, then d must also divide a - q*b for any integer q. When we take q = floor(a/b), then a - q*b = a mod b. Therefore, the set of common divisors of (a, b) is exactly the same as the set of common divisors of (b, a mod b). This means gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). The algorithm based on this: Given a and b, repeatedly replace (a, b) with (b, a mod b) until b becomes 0, then a is the GCD. Time complexity analysis shows each step reduces the larger number significantly. Space complexity for the conceptual algorithm is O(1) if implemented iteratively, but we're building toward recursion. This feeds into the key subroutine by providing the mathematical justification for the recursive calls. Common mistakes include not understanding why this property holds, or confusing it with other GCD properties. There's no direct code here—it's the mathematical foundation. Example reasoning: For a=48, b=18: gcd(48,18) = gcd(18,48 mod 18=12) = gcd(12,18 mod 12=6) = gcd(6,12 mod 6=0) = 6.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with (36, 24)
Step 2: Compute 36 mod 24 = 12
Step 3: By Euclidean property: gcd(36, 24) = gcd(24, 12)
Step 4: Now we have smaller numbers (24, 12)
Note: This is one application of the property

Step 1: Start with (105, 45)
Step 2: Compute 105 mod 45 = 15
Step 3: By Euclidean property: gcd(105, 45) = gcd(45, 15)
Step 4: Numbers reduced from (105, 45) to (45, 15)

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation for Reduction
ladders.desc: Using a % b to obtain remainder for reducing GCD problem size
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the modulo operation and how it helps reduce the GCD problem. The intuition is that when finding gcd(a, b), we don't need the exact quotient—we only need the remainder a % b because it contains the essential divisibility information. The remainder is always smaller than b, which guarantees progress toward the base case. The algorithm: Given two numbers a and b, compute r = a % b. This remainder r has the property that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, r). Time complexity of modulo operation is O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by providing the computational tool that implements the Euclidean property. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division, not handling negative mod results (in some languages), or not understanding that a % b when b > a simply returns a. Real code pattern: remainder = a % b. For learning purposes, we can implement modulo manually: while a >= b: a = a - b, then remainder = a.

examples.trace:
Step 1: 48 divided by 18 equals 2 with remainder
Step 2: 18 * 2 = 36
Step 3: 48 - 36 = 12
Step 4: Therefore, 48 % 18 = 12

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Remainder
ladders.desc: Fundamental arithmetic operations: division (//) and modulo (%)
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the fundamental arithmetic needed for the Euclidean algorithm. The intuition is that when we divide integer a by integer b, we get a quotient (how many times b fits completely into a) and a remainder (what's left over). Formally: a = b * q + r, where 0 ≤ r < b. The modulo operation (%) gives us r, while integer division (//) gives us q. Understanding this relationship is crucial because the Euclidean algorithm uses the remainder to reduce the problem. The algorithm: For integers a and b, compute q = a // b and r = a % b such that a = b*q + r. Time and space complexity are O(1) for basic arithmetic. This feeds into the building block by providing the complete division operation—the building block uses just the remainder part. Common mistakes include confusing integer division with float division, not understanding the range of remainder (0 ≤ r < b), or not seeing the relationship between quotient and remainder. Real code pattern: q = a // b, r = a % b.

examples.trace:
Step 1: How many times does 18 fit into 48? 18*2=36, 18*3=54 (too big)
Step 2: So quotient q = 2
Step 3: Remainder r = 48 - 18*2 = 48 - 36 = 12
Step 4: Verify: 48 = 18*2 + 12

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest Common Divisor Definition
ladders.desc: Understanding what GCD means: largest integer dividing both numbers
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the very definition of greatest common divisor (GCD). The intuition is simple: given two integers, we want to find the biggest number that divides both of them evenly (with zero remainder). For example, for numbers 12 and 18, the common divisors are 1, 2, 3, and 6. The greatest is 6, so gcd(12, 18) = 6. The algorithm to understand this concept: 1. List all divisors of the first number. 2. List all divisors of the second number. 3. Find numbers that appear in both lists (common divisors). 4. Pick the largest one. Time complexity for this naive approach is O(min(a, b)) if we check all numbers up to the smaller number. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the basic operation by establishing what we're ultimately trying to compute—all the higher levels are more efficient ways to find this same result. Common mistakes include confusing GCD with LCM (least common multiple), not understanding that every number has at least 1 as a divisor, or thinking GCD can be larger than the numbers themselves. Real code pattern for understanding: def naive_gcd(a, b): result = 1; for i in range(1, min(a,b)+1): if a%i==0 and b%i==0: result = i; return result.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Divisors of 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12
Step 2: Divisors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18
Step 3: Common divisors: 1, 2, 3, 6
Step 4: Greatest common divisor: 6

Step 1: Divisors of 8: 1, 2, 4, 8
Step 2: Divisors of 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12
Step 3: Common divisors: 1, 2, 4
Step 4: Greatest common divisor: 4

approaches.name: Euclidean Algorithm Iterative O(log(min(a,b)))
approaches.summary: Implement the Euclidean algorithm iteratively using a while loop to repeatedly replace a with b and b with a % b until b becomes 0, then return a. This avoids recursion overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative GCD Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of GCD using iterative Euclidean algorithm
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires implementing the Euclidean algorithm iteratively to find the greatest common divisor of two integers. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the mathematical property that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This property allows us to repeatedly reduce the problem size until we reach a base case where b = 0, at which point a contains the GCD. The iterative approach uses a while loop to repeatedly update a and b: set a = b and b = a % b until b becomes 0. This avoids recursion overhead and uses constant space. The time complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) because each iteration reduces the numbers significantly (at least by a factor related to the golden ratio). The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) by implementing the complete loop structure. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (should take absolute values), infinite loops if not updating variables correctly, and edge cases with zero (gcd(a, 0) = |a|). The real code pattern involves initializing variables, entering a while loop that continues while b != 0, and performing the modulo operation and swaps.

examples.trace: Step 1: a = 48, b = 18, b != 0, continue. Step 2: temp = a % b = 48 % 18 = 12. Step 3: a = b = 18, b = temp = 12. Step 4: b != 0, continue. Step 5: temp = a % b = 18 % 12 = 6. Step 6: a = b = 12, b = temp = 6. Step 7: b != 0, continue. Step 8: temp = a % b = 12 % 6 = 0. Step 9: a = b = 6, b = temp = 0. Step 10: b == 0, exit loop. Return a = 6.

Step 1: a = 101, b = 103, b != 0, continue. Step 2: temp = a % b = 101 % 103 = 101 (since 101 < 103). Step 3: a = b = 103, b = temp = 101. Step 4: b != 0, continue. Step 5: temp = a % b = 103 % 101 = 2. Step 6: a = b = 101, b = temp = 2. Step 7: b != 0, continue. Step 8: temp = a % b = 101 % 2 = 1. Step 9: a = b = 2, b = temp = 1. Step 10: b != 0, continue. Step 11: temp = a % b = 2 % 1 = 0. Step 12: a = b = 1, b = temp = 0. Step 13: b == 0, exit loop. Return a = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — While Loop with Modulo Update
ladders.desc: Implement the core while loop that repeatedly applies a % b and swaps values
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the while loop that forms the heart of the iterative Euclidean algorithm. The intuition is that by continuously replacing the larger number with the remainder of division, we reduce the problem size while preserving the GCD. The algorithm starts with two numbers a and b. While b is not zero, we compute a % b (the remainder when a is divided by b), then update a to b and b to the remainder. This process repeats until b becomes 0, at which point a holds the GCD. The time complexity for this loop is O(log(min(a, b))) because each iteration reduces the numbers significantly (the remainder is always less than b, so the numbers decrease quickly). Space complexity is O(1) as we only modify variables in place. This feeds into the full problem (L0) by providing the iterative mechanism to compute GCD. Common mistakes include not swapping variables correctly (leading to incorrect updates), infinite loops if the condition is wrong, and not handling the case when b is initially 0. The real code pattern uses a while b != 0 loop with temporary variables for the modulo operation and assignment.

examples.trace: Step 1: b = 12 != 0, enter loop. Step 2: temp = a % b = 30 % 12 = 6. Step 3: a = b = 12, b = temp = 6. Step 4: b = 6 != 0, continue. Step 5: temp = a % b = 12 % 6 = 0. Step 6: a = b = 6, b = temp = 0. Step 7: b = 0, exit loop. Final: a = 6, b = 0.

Step 1: b = 3 != 0, enter loop. Step 2: temp = a % b = 7 % 3 = 1. Step 3: a = b = 3, b = temp = 1. Step 4: b = 1 != 0, continue. Step 5: temp = a % b = 3 % 1 = 0. Step 6: a = b = 1, b = temp = 0. Step 7: b = 0, exit loop. Final: a = 1, b = 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Euclidean Algorithm Property: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b)
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical property that reduces GCD computation
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core mathematical insight behind the Euclidean algorithm: for any two integers a and b (with b != 0), gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). The intuition is that any common divisor of a and b must also divide a - q*b for any integer q, and specifically when q = floor(a/b), a - q*b is exactly a % b. Therefore, the set of common divisors remains unchanged, allowing us to replace the larger number with the remainder. This property enables recursive or iterative reduction until we reach gcd(d, 0) = d. The algorithm involves repeatedly applying this property: start with a and b, compute r = a % b, then set a = b and b = r, and repeat. The time complexity arises from how quickly the remainder reduces; it's O(log(min(a, b))) because each step reduces the numbers by a factor related to the golden ratio. Space is O(1) if done iteratively. This connects to the key sub-routine (L1) by providing the logical basis for the while loop updates. Common mistakes include not understanding why this property holds, misapplying it with negative numbers, or not handling the base case when b = 0. The real code pattern is based on this property to drive the iterative updates.

examples.trace: Step 1: gcd(56, 98). Since 98 > 56, swap: gcd(98, 56). Step 2: Apply property: gcd(98, 56) = gcd(56, 98 % 56) = gcd(56, 42). Step 3: Apply property: gcd(56, 42) = gcd(42, 56 % 42) = gcd(42, 14). Step 4: Apply property: gcd(42, 14) = gcd(14, 42 % 14) = gcd(14, 0). Step 5: Base case: gcd(14, 0) = 14. So GCD is 14.

Step 1: gcd(27, 15). Apply property: gcd(27, 15) = gcd(15, 27 % 15) = gcd(15, 12). Step 2: Apply property: gcd(15, 12) = gcd(12, 15 % 12) = gcd(12, 3). Step 3: Apply property: gcd(12, 3) = gcd(3, 12 % 3) = gcd(3, 0). Step 4: Base case: gcd(3, 0) = 3. So GCD is 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Compute remainder (a % b) and understand its properties for GCD
ladders.explanation: This level involves understanding and implementing the modulo operation, which computes the remainder when a is divided by b. For integers a and b (b > 0), a % b gives the remainder r such that a = q*b + r, where 0 ≤ r < b. This operation is crucial for the Euclidean algorithm because it reduces the size of numbers while preserving GCD. The intuition is that the remainder r = a % b shares the same GCD with b as a does with b, due to the linear combination property. The algorithm for modulo can be implemented using integer division: r = a - (a // b) * b, where // is floor division. Time complexity is O(1) for basic arithmetic, and space is O(1). This connects to the core logic (L2) by providing the computational tool to apply the Euclidean property. Common mistakes include confusion with negative numbers (different languages handle modulo differently; in Python, a % b always returns a non-negative result less than b), division by zero, and not understanding the relationship between quotient and remainder. The real code pattern uses the % operator or manual computation with division.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute quotient q = 17 // 5 = 3 (integer division). Step 2: Compute remainder r = 17 - q * 5 = 17 - 3*5 = 17 - 15 = 2. Step 3: Verify: 17 = 3*5 + 2, and 0 ≤ 2 < 5. So 17 % 5 = 2.
Step 1: Compute quotient q = 10 // 3 = 3 (since 3*3 = 9 ≤ 10). Step 2: Compute remainder r = 10 - q * 3 = 10 - 3*3 = 10 - 9 = 1. Step 3: Verify: 10 = 3*3 + 1, and 0 ≤ 1 < 3. So 10 % 3 = 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Variable Assignment and Swapping
ladders.desc: Assign values to variables and swap two variables using a temporary variable
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations of assigning values to variables and swapping the values of two variables. In programming, variables are used to store data, and assignment sets a variable to a specific value. Swapping two variables, say a and b, requires a temporary variable to hold one value during the exchange to avoid data loss. The intuition is that direct assignment like a = b would overwrite a's original value, so we use temp = a, a = b, b = temp. This is essential in the Euclidean algorithm where we repeatedly update a and b with new values. The algorithm for swapping involves three steps: store a in temp, assign b to a, then assign temp to b. Time and space complexity are both O(1) as it involves a constant number of operations and uses one extra variable. This connects to the building block (L3) by enabling the updates in the modulo operation loop. Common mistakes include forgetting the temporary variable (leading to incorrect swaps), using incorrect order of assignments, or not initializing variables properly. The real code pattern uses simple assignment statements and a temp variable.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize a = 5, b = 10. Step 2: Create temp variable: temp = a = 5. Step 3: Assign a = b: a = 10. Step 4: Assign b = temp: b = 5. Step 5: Result: a = 10, b = 5.
Step 1: Initialize a = 3, b = 7. Step 2: Create temp variable: temp = a = 3. Step 3: Assign a = b: a = 7. Step 4: Assign b = temp: b = 3. Step 5: Result: a = 7, b = 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what GCD is and how to find it for small numbers by listing factors
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the concept of the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers. The GCD is the largest positive integer that divides both numbers without leaving a remainder. For example, the GCD of 12 and 18 is 6 because 6 is the largest number that divides both 12 and 18. The intuition is that factors of a number are integers that divide it evenly, and the GCD is the greatest factor common to both numbers. To find GCD for small numbers, one can list all factors of each number and identify the largest common one. For a, list all integers from 1 to a that divide a; similarly for b. Then, find the intersection of these lists and pick the maximum. This method is straightforward but inefficient for large numbers, leading to the need for algorithms like Euclidean. Time complexity is O(min(a, b)) in worst case, space is O(min(a, b)) to store factors. This connects to the basic operation (L4) by establishing the goal of GCD computation. Common mistakes include confusing GCD with LCM (least common multiple), not considering 1 as always a common divisor, or missing negative numbers (GCD is defined for non-negative integers, typically taken as absolute values). The real code pattern involves loops to find factors and compare them.

examples.trace: Step 1: Factors of 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. Step 2: Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18. Step 3: Common factors: 1, 2, 3, 6. Step 4: Greatest common factor: 6. So GCD(12, 18) = 6.
Step 1: Factors of 7: 1, 7. Step 2: Factors of 5: 1, 5. Step 3: Common factors: 1. Step 4: Greatest common factor: 1. So GCD(7, 5) = 1.
